CRM2026

The CRM Trap: When Systems of Record Never Become Systems of Action.

CRM becomes valuable when it helps the enterprise decide what happens next. Recording activity is not enough.

Most CRM programs do not fail because the enterprise picked the wrong platform. They fail because the platform never becomes part of how work actually moves.

The system captures records. It stores activity. It gives leaders reporting. But the work still lives in email, meetings, spreadsheets, swivel-chair processes, local judgment, and informal escalation paths.

A system of record tells the enterprise what happened. A system of action changes what happens next.

The record is not the work

Recording effort is not the same as improving work. If CRM does not clarify ownership, guide follow-up, surface risk, or make the next action easier, adoption becomes a compliance exercise.

People enter data because they have to. Leaders get dashboards. The customer still feels the seams.

CRM data is often work exhaust

Activity data, notes, service history, ownership, relationship plans, and next steps are often captured after the work happened. That makes CRM useful for reporting, but weaker as a decision system.

The opportunity is to turn CRM data into decision context: what matters now, what is unresolved, who owns it, and what action should happen next.

Where value leaks

Value leaks when customer context, workflow status, decision rights, and operating controls sit in different places. The associate has one view. The advisor has another. The manager has a dashboard. The client experiences the gaps.

Modernization has to focus on the movement of work, not the beauty of the application.

What good looks like

CRM becomes useful when it helps the enterprise route work, prioritize action, capture evidence, reduce rework, and improve the quality of customer and associate experience.

That requires data discipline, workflow design, adoption, governance, and a clear view of what outcome the system is meant to improve.

The executive test

The question is not whether the CRM has been deployed. The question is whether it has changed how work gets done.

Author

Chandra Kanojia

Enterprise AI and transformation operator focused on data readiness, CRM/Salesforce, workflow, regulated operating models, and trusted execution.
